sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A health psychology lab tracked 150 undergraduates during a week of midterms. Students were grouped by average nightly sleep: under 6 hours, 6–7.5 hours, and over 7.5 hours. Researchers measured psychomotor vigilance task (PVT) lapses, self-reported stress on a 1–10 scale, and quiz scores out of 20. Mean PVT lapses were 14, 8, and 5; stress scores were 8.2, 6.7, and 5.8; quiz scores were 12.6, 15.1, and 16.3. Students in the shortest-sleep group were also the most likely to report late-night caffeine use and skipped meals.
questions.stem: Compared with the shortest-sleep group, students sleeping more than 7.5 hours showed which pattern in Figure 1?

choices.D: Fewer vigilance lapses and higher quiz scores
choices.A: More vigilance lapses and lower quiz scores
choices.B: Fewer lapses but lower quiz scores
choices.C: More lapses but higher quiz scores

explanations.D: Correct. Students sleeping more than 7.5 hours had 5 lapses and a 16.3 quiz average, compared with 14 lapses and a 12.6 quiz average in the under-6-hours group. The figure therefore shows both better vigilance and better academic performance with longer sleep.
explanations.A: The direction of both variables is reversed relative to the figure.
explanations.B: Quiz scores rose rather than fell in the longer-sleep group.
explanations.C: Lapses decreased, not increased, with longer sleep.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Psychomotor lapses and quiz scores across three sleep-duration groups during midterms.

questions.stem: The association between less sleep and poorer vigilance most directly supports which concept?

choices.C: Sleep loss impairs attention and executive control

explanations.C: Correct. PVT lapses are a classic indicator of sustained attention. The higher lapse count among shorter-sleep students fits a large literature showing that insufficient sleep undermines attentional control and moment-to-moment performance.
explanations.A: James-Lange theory concerns bodily arousal and emotion, not vigilance under sleep restriction.
explanations.B: Role conflict is a sociological concept about competing statuses, not a cognitive consequence of sleep loss.
explanations.D: Reference-group theory concerns social comparison, not attention.

questions.stem: Which variable is a likely confounder when interpreting the relationship between short sleep and quiz scores?

choices.B: Late-night caffeine use and skipped meals
choices.A: The fact that quiz scores were measured out of 20
choices.C: The names of the midterm courses

explanations.B: Correct. Because the study grouped students by naturally occurring sleep habits, other behaviors could differ across groups. The shortest-sleep group also used more caffeine and skipped meals, so those factors could contribute to the poorer quiz outcomes.
explanations.A: The score scale does not create a competing explanation for group differences.
explanations.C: Course names alone do not explain the observed sleep-related pattern.
explanations.D: Using PVT lapses is part of the measurement strategy, not a confound.

questions.stem: If a student wants to improve performance based on these data, which change is most evidence-consistent?

choices.A: Increase average sleep duration rather than relying on late-night wakefulness
choices.B: Study all night and add more caffeine
choices.C: Skip breakfast to save time for studying
choices.D: Focus only on reducing stress without considering sleep

explanations.A: Correct. The longest-sleep group showed the best vigilance and quiz performance. Although the study is observational, the pattern strongly suggests that protecting sleep is more consistent with good performance than trading sleep for extra late-night work.
explanations.B: The shortest-sleep group already resembled this pattern and performed the worst.
explanations.C: Skipping meals appeared alongside poorer outcomes, not better ones.
explanations.D: Stress matters, but the data specifically highlight sleep duration as a key correlate of performance.

questions.stem: Why can the data not establish that short sleep alone caused lower quiz scores?

choices.D: Students were not randomly assigned to sleep durations, so other differences may account for part of the score gap
choices.A: Quiz scores were measured numerically rather than categorically
choices.B: The study included three sleep groups instead of two
choices.C: The figure also reported vigilance lapses

explanations.D: Correct. This is an observational comparison. Students who sleep less may differ in workload, health habits, or baseline anxiety, so the relationship is consistent with an effect of sleep but does not isolate sleep as the sole causal factor.
explanations.A: The numeric format of quiz scores does not prevent causal inference.
explanations.B: Having three groups can actually be informative; it is not the core causal limitation.
explanations.C: Including another outcome variable does not explain why causation cannot be proven.

questions.passage: A teaching hospital offered residents one of three optional stress-management programs during an 8-week rotation: a mindfulness group, a problem-focused time-management workshop, or no workshop. Burnout scores were measured on a 0–50 scale before and after the rotation; lower scores indicate less burnout. Mean burnout changed from 29 to 24 in the mindfulness group, from 30 to 26 in the time-management group, and from 28 to 31 in the no-workshop group. Residents in the mindfulness group also reported the largest improvement in sleep quality, whereas time-management participants reported the greatest reduction in missed deadlines.
questions.stem: Which group showed the greatest reduction in burnout across the rotation?

explanations.C: Correct. The mindfulness group declined from 29 to 24, a 5-point reduction. Time management declined by 4 points, and the no-workshop group increased by 3 points, so mindfulness produced the largest improvement.
explanations.A: Time management improved burnout, but the change was slightly smaller than in the mindfulness group.
explanations.B: The no-workshop group worsened rather than improved.
explanations.D: The groups changed in different directions and by different amounts.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Mean burnout scores before and after an 8-week rotation in three resident groups.

questions.stem: The time-management workshop is best classified as which coping strategy?

explanations.B: Correct. Time management targets the stressor itself by helping residents organize tasks and reduce missed deadlines. That is the hallmark of problem-focused coping: attempting to change a manageable source of stress.
explanations.A: Emotion-focused coping aims primarily to regulate feelings rather than alter the practical problem.
explanations.C: Displacement is a defense mechanism, not a planned coping strategy taught in the workshop.
explanations.D: Somatization refers to expressing distress through physical symptoms.

questions.stem: Which detail most specifically supports a distinct mechanism for the mindfulness group?

choices.A: Residents in that group reported the largest improvement in sleep quality
choices.B: Residents in all groups were working in a teaching hospital
choices.C: The no-workshop group began with a burnout score of 28
choices.D: Burnout was measured on a 0–50 scale

explanations.A: Correct. Mindfulness reduced burnout and was uniquely paired with the largest gain in sleep quality, which supports a mechanism related to reduced arousal and improved regulation rather than simply improved scheduling.
explanations.B: The shared hospital setting does not distinguish mechanisms between interventions.
explanations.C: The starting score of the control group does not reveal why mindfulness worked.
explanations.D: The measurement scale describes the instrument but not the mechanism.

questions.stem: A resident who mainly feels overwhelmed by a chaotic task list would, based on the passage, be most likely to benefit from which option?

choices.B: A strategy that ignores deadlines and focuses only on suppression
choices.C: Only mindfulness, because practical strategies never help

explanations.D: Correct. The passage notes that time-management participants showed the greatest reduction in missed deadlines, suggesting that the workshop specifically helped residents handle modifiable task demands. That makes it the most aligned option for a chaotic schedule problem.
explanations.A: The no-workshop group worsened on burnout and offers no structured response to the stated problem.
explanations.B: Suppression alone does not address the practical source of overload.
explanations.C: Mindfulness helped overall, but the passage specifically links time management to reduced deadline problems.

questions.stem: What is the main limitation in interpreting these workshop differences?

choices.C: Residents chose whether to participate, so self-selection could influence the results
choices.B: Mindfulness and time management targeted different skills
choices.D: The scale used lower numbers to indicate less burnout

explanations.C: Correct. Because the programs were optional, residents who selected one workshop may have differed in motivation, schedule, or baseline coping style. Those differences could contribute to the outcome pattern alongside the interventions themselves.
explanations.A: Pre/post measurement is useful and is not the main threat here.
explanations.B: Different workshop content helps explain mechanisms, but the core limitation is nonrandom participation.
explanations.D: The direction of the scale does not create the interpretive problem.
